summary: I served as academic dean at Miles College before entering the political arena in Birmingham. During my time there, Lucius Pitts was the president, and I gained exposure to community leaders and citizen groups through my work. This connection helped me when I decided to run for city council in 1971, as I was not completely unknown in the Birmingham area. My role at Miles College was a significant part of my background and helped me build relationships that supported my political efforts.

I came to the Alabama Center for Higher Education in 1970, which marked the beginning of my involvement in politics. It was while working here that I was approached by young black activists who encouraged me to run for office in Birmingham. This organization was the setting where I transitioned from academia into political life, leading to my first run for city council in 1971. The Center was pivotal in my career shift and political awakening.

When I decided to run for city council in 1971, I did not initially consult with the Progressive Democrats of Jefferson County, but I believed I would receive their support. Indeed, I was endorsed by them on the ballot, which was important for my campaign. Their endorsement reflected the political climate and the desire for new faces and ideas within the black community in Birmingham at that time. The Progressive Democrats played a key role in supporting black political candidates like myself.

Although I was born in Livingston, Alabama, I consider myself a native of Birmingham because I have lived here since I was four years old. Birmingham is where I grew up, attended school, and later worked in higher education and politics. It is also the city where I first ran for political office in 1971 and became involved in community issues like police brutality and racial tensions. Birmingham has been central to my personal and professional life.

Lucius Pitts was the president of Miles College while I was serving as academic dean there. He was a well-known black leader in the Birmingham community and was very active in local affairs. Dr. Pitts involved me in important community initiatives, such as drafting documents for the community action committee addressing police brutality. His leadership and our collaboration helped me gain exposure to citizen groups and prepared me for my political career.

Operation New Birmingham is a powerful organization composed mainly of civic leaders and businessmen in the city. I believe it has done more good than harm by helping to improve communication and address racial tensions in Birmingham. However, it has sometimes made awkward moves, such as its involvement in the last city election through the Birmingham Action Group, which left a bitter taste among many black citizens. Despite criticisms about its exclusivity and sensitivity to criticism, Operation New Birmingham has played an important role in the city's progress.

The Birmingham Action Group, or BAG, was connected to Operation New Birmingham and played a role in the last city election. Although officials denied it, it was clear to me that Operation New Birmingham influenced the re-election of incumbents through BAG. The tactics used were subtle but appealed to racial sentiments, which caused resentment in the black community. BAG's involvement highlighted some of the challenges in Birmingham's political landscape during that time.

David Vann was a veteran politician and fellow candidate when I ran for city council in 1971. He provided me with advice and support, especially during the runoff election. Vann later became a member of the Birmingham city council himself. His guidance was valuable to me as I navigated my first political campaign in Birmingham.

I was originally from Livingston, Alabama, a town in the black belt region where my parents were sharecroppers. I lived there until I was four years old before my family moved to Birmingham for work opportunities. Although I left Livingston at a young age, it is part of my roots and family history. My upbringing in Livingston contrasts with my life and career in Birmingham.

U. C. Clemon is not directly mentioned in my story, but as a prominent figure in Birmingham's political and legal community, he represents the kind of leadership that has influenced the city's progress. Like myself, he has been involved in efforts to advance equality and representation for black citizens in Birmingham. His role complements the broader political and social changes taking place during my time in office.
